subject: Physics
q: A point like object is placed at distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in front of a convex lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. A plane mirror is placed at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> behind the lens. The position and nature of the image formed by the system is
question: A point like object is placed at distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in front of a convex lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. A plane mirror is placed at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> behind the lens. The position and nature of the image formed by the system is
solution:
For I1 applying lens formula

1v1-1-1=10.5

v1=1

For I2 applying condition of plane mirror

for final image (I3) again applying lens formula

1v3-1-3=10.5

v2=35

The distance of final image from mirror

D=35+3

D=2.6

The image will be real (but it is given virtual in answer)

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens made of glass (refractive index $=1.5$ ) has focal length 24 cm in air. When it is totally immersed in water (refractive index $=1.33$ ), its focal length changes to
question: A convex lens made of glass (refractive index $=1.5$ ) has focal length 24 cm in air. When it is totally immersed in water (refractive index $=1.33$ ), its focal length changes to
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{8}=\left(\frac{\mu_{\ell}}{\mu_{\mathrm{s}}}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right] \\
& \frac{1}{24}=(1.5-1)\left[\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}\right] ...(i)\\
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.33}-1\right)\left(\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}\right) \\
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}}=\left(\frac{1.5 \times 3}{4}-1\right) \frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}...(ii)
\end{aligned}$
(i) divided by (ii)
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}}{24}=4 \\
& \mathrm{f}^{\prime}=96 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A plano-convex lens becomes an optical system of 28 cm focal length when its plane surface is silvered and illuminated from left to right as shown in fig-A If the same lens is instead silvered on the curved surface and illuminated from another side as in fig-B, it acts as an optical system of focal length 10 cm. The refractive index of the material of the lens is:
question: A plano-convex lens becomes an optical system of 28 cm focal length when its plane surface is silvered and illuminated from left to right as shown in fig-A If the same lens is instead silvered on the curved surface and illuminated from another side as in fig-B, it acts as an optical system of focal length 10 cm. The refractive index of the material of the lens is:
solution:
Case-1



1 f 1 = (μ−1) R ,f=-28 ⇒P=2p1+p2
⇒128=2μ-1R

Case-2



1f1=(μ−1)R, f2=R2 , f =−10 cm
⇒P=2P1+P2⇒110=2μ-12+2R⇒110=228+2R

2R=110-228=18280⇒R=2809 cm⇒128=2μ-12809

μ-1=59⇒μ=1+59=149=1.55.

subject: Physics
q: We wish to make a microscope with the help of two positive lenses both with a focal length of 20 $\mathrm{mm}$ each and the object is positioned $25 \mathrm{~mm}$ from the objective lens. How far apart the lenses should be so that the final image is formed at infinity?
question: We wish to make a microscope with the help of two positive lenses both with a focal length of 20 $\mathrm{mm}$ each and the object is positioned $25 \mathrm{~mm}$ from the objective lens. How far apart the lenses should be so that the final image is formed at infinity?
solution: To obtain final image at infinity, object which is the image formed by objective should be at focal distance of eye-piece. By lens formula (for objective) $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{f_0} \\ \text { or, } \quad & \frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{-25}=\frac{1}{20} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{1}{v_0}=\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{25}=\frac{5-4}{100}=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{~mm} \\ \therefore \quad & v_0=100 \mathrm{~mm} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore the distance between the lenses $$ =v_0+f_e=100 \mathrm{~mm}+20 \mathrm{~mm}=120 \mathrm{~mm} $$

question: Two plane mirrors M1 and M2 are at right angle to each other shown. A point source P is placed at a and 2a meter away from M1 and M2 respectively. The shortest distance between the images thus formed is : (Take 5=2.3)
solution: We know that the formula of the distance between two points is, d=x2-x12+y2-y12, now put the values from the above the diagram in the above formula, then the distance between I1 and I2, d=(4a)2+(2a)2≈4.6a

subject: Physics
q: A ray of light is incident from air on a glass plate having thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. The angle of incidence of a ray is equal to the critical angle for glass-air interface. The lateral displacement of the ray when it passes through the plate is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><msup><mn>10</mn><mo>-</mo></msup><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>cm</mi></math> . (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mo> </mo><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn></math>)
question: A ray of light is incident from air on a glass plate having thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. The angle of incidence of a ray is equal to the critical angle for glass-air interface. The lateral displacement of the ray when it passes through the plate is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><msup><mn>10</mn><mo>-</mo></msup><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>cm</mi></math> . (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mo> </mo><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn></math>)
solution:
Let C be the critical angle.

Then, sinC=1μ, where, μ is the refractive index of glass.

So, sinC=12⇒C=45°

As the angle of incidence is equal to the critical angle, so using Snell's law,

 sinC=μsinθ

⇒12=2 sinθ

⇒θ=30°

Now, lateral displacement of the ray is 

x=t sin(i-r) sec r

⇒x=3sin 45°-30°sec 30°

⇒x=3(0.26)23

⇒x=0.52 cm

⇒x=52×10-2 cm

subject: Physics
q:
A ray of light entering from air into a denser medium of refractive index 43, as shown in figure.
The light ray suffers total internal reflection at the adjacent surface as shown. The maximum value of angle θ should be equal to:
question:
A ray of light entering from air into a denser medium of refractive index 43, as shown in figure.
The light ray suffers total internal reflection at the adjacent surface as shown. The maximum value of angle θ should be equal to:
solution:
At maximum angle θ ray at point B goes in gazing emergence, at all less values of θ, TIR occurs. At point B

43×sinθ''=1×sin90°

θ''=sin-134

θ'=π2-θ''

At point A

1×sinθ=43×sinθ'

sinθ=43×sinπ2-θ''

sinθ=43coscos-174

sinθ=43×74

θ=sin-173

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R)
Assertion (A) : Refractive index of glass is higher than that of air.
Reason ( $\mathrm{R}$) : Optical density of a medium is directly proportionate to its mass density which results in a proportionate refractive index.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R)
Assertion (A) : Refractive index of glass is higher than that of air.
Reason ( $\mathrm{R}$) : Optical density of a medium is directly proportionate to its mass density which results in a proportionate refractive index.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: $(\mathrm{A})$ is not correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is correct
Both $(\mathrm{A})$ and $(\mathrm{R})$ are correct and $(\mathrm{R})$ is the correct explanation of (A)
$(A)$ is correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is not correct
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Refractive index has no relation with mass density because both have different meaning. Hence reason is incorrect.
So (A) is correct but (R) is not correct.

subject: Physics
q:
In the given figure, the face AC of the equilateral prism is immersed in a liquid of refractive index n. For incident angle 60° at the side AC, the refracted light beam just grazes along face AC. The refractive index of the liquid n=x4. The value of x is _____ .
(Given refractive index of glass =1.5)
question:
In the given figure, the face AC of the equilateral prism is immersed in a liquid of refractive index n. For incident angle 60° at the side AC, the refracted light beam just grazes along face AC. The refractive index of the liquid n=x4. The value of x is _____ .
(Given refractive index of glass =1.5)
solution:
When the refracted light just grazes the boundary the incident angle is called a critical angle.

Using snell's law at face AC.

1.5sin60°=n×sin90° 

⇒1.5×32=n=x4 

⇒33=x

⇒x=27

subject: Physics
q: For a thin symmetric prism made of glass (refractive index $1.5$), the ratio of incident angle and minimum deviation will be _______.
question: For a thin symmetric prism made of glass (refractive index $1.5$), the ratio of incident angle and minimum deviation will be _______.
solution:
For a thin prism, the angle of minimum deviation is given by $\delta_m = (\mu - 1)A$.

At the position of minimum deviation, the angle of incidence $i$ and angle of emergence $e$ are equal, and the angle of refraction is $r = \dfrac{A}{2}$.

Using Snell's law for small angles, $i = \mu r = \mu \left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)$.

The ratio of the incident angle to the minimum deviation is:

$\dfrac{i}{\delta_m} = \dfrac{\dfrac{\mu A}{2}}{(\mu - 1)A} = \dfrac{\mu}{2(\mu - 1)}$

Given $\mu = 1.5$, substituting the value:

$\dfrac{i}{\delta_m} = \dfrac{1.5}{2(1.5 - 1)} = \dfrac{1.5}{2(0.5)} = \dfrac{1.5}{1} = \dfrac{3}{2}$

Answer: $3 : 2$

subject: Physics
q: Critical angle of incidence for a pair of optical media is $45^{\circ}$. The refractive indices of first and second media are in the ratio:
question: Critical angle of incidence for a pair of optical media is $45^{\circ}$. The refractive indices of first and second media are in the ratio:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \sin \theta_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{R}}}{\mu_{\mathrm{d}}}=\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1} \\ & \sin 45^{\circ}=\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Y</mi></math> plane be taken as the boundary between two transparent media <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has a refractive index of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo><</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has a refractive index of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>. A ray of light travelling in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> along the direction given by the vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn><mi>i</mi></msqrt><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mi>j</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, is incident on the plane of separation. The value of difference between the angle of incident in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and the angle of refraction in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be _____ degree.
question: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Y</mi></math> plane be taken as the boundary between two transparent media <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has a refractive index of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo><</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has a refractive index of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>. A ray of light travelling in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> along the direction given by the vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn><mi>i</mi></msqrt><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mi>j</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, is incident on the plane of separation. The value of difference between the angle of incident in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and the angle of refraction in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be _____ degree.
solution:
Given: A→=43i-33j-5k^

As incident vector A makes i angle with normal Z-axis and refracted vector R makes r angle with normal Z- axis.

The angle made by the incident ray with the normal is given as 

i=cos-1AzA=cos-15(43)2+(33)2+52

=cos-1510⇒i=60°

Applying Snell's law of refraction at the interface of two media 

μ1sini=μ2sinr

⇒2sin60°=3×sinr

⇒r=45°

Difference between i and r =60°-45°=15°

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens of focal length 30 cm is placed in contact with a concave lens of focal length 20 cm. An object is placed at 20 cm to the left of this lens system. The distance of the image from the lens in cm is ________
question: A convex lens of focal length 30 cm is placed in contact with a concave lens of focal length 20 cm. An object is placed at 20 cm to the left of this lens system. The distance of the image from the lens in cm is ________
solution:
Equivalent focal length
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2} \\ & =\frac{1}{30}+\frac{1}{-20}=\frac{2-3}{60}=-\frac{1}{60} \\ & f=-60 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$
Lens formula
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{-20}=\frac{1}{-60} \\ & \mathrm{v}=-15 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A concave mirror produces an image of an object such that the distance between the object and image is 20 cm . If the magnification of the image is ' -3 ', then the magnitude of the radius of curvature of the mirror is :
question: A concave mirror produces an image of an object such that the distance between the object and image is 20 cm . If the magnification of the image is ' -3 ', then the magnitude of the radius of curvature of the mirror is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & m=-3=-\frac{u}{v} \\ & u=-x \\ & v=-3 x \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x=20 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & x=10 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & f=\frac{u V}{u+V}=\frac{(-10)(-30)}{(-10)+(-30)}=-7.5 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & f=-\frac{R}{2} \\ & R=-2 f=15 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
In a medium the speed of light wave decreases to 0.2 times to its speed in free space. The ratio of relative permittivity to the refractive index of the medium is x:1. The value of x is ______.

(Given speed of light in free space =3×108 m s-1 and for the given medium μr=1)
question:
In a medium the speed of light wave decreases to 0.2 times to its speed in free space. The ratio of relative permittivity to the refractive index of the medium is x:1. The value of x is ______.

(Given speed of light in free space =3×108 m s-1 and for the given medium μr=1)
solution:
Speed of light in medium is related to speed of light in vacuum c and refractive index μ by v=cμ.

Then, we have μ=cv=c0.2c

⇒μ=5

Refractive index in terms of relative permeability μrand relative permittivityεr  of the medium is stated as μ=ϵr μr

⇒ϵr=μ2μr

∴ ϵrμ=μμr=5

Hence, the value of x=5.

q: A thin glass (refractive index 1.5) lens has optical power of $-5 D$ in air. Its optical power in a liquid medium with refractive index $1.6$ will be
question: A thin glass (refractive index 1.5) lens has optical power of $-5 D$ in air. Its optical power in a liquid medium with refractive index $1.6$ will be
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{air}}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\mu_{\ell}}{\mu_{\mathrm{a}}}-1\right)}{\left(\frac{\mu_{\ell}}{\mu_{\mathrm{m}}}-1\right)}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{m}}=5 / 8 \mathrm{D}$

subject: Physics
q: An object 'O' is placed at a distance of 100 cm in front of a concave mirror of radius of curvature 200 cm as shown in the figure. The object starts moving towards the mirror at a speed 2 cm s-1. The position of the image from the mirror after 10 s will be at _____ cm.
question: An object 'O' is placed at a distance of 100 cm in front of a concave mirror of radius of curvature 200 cm as shown in the figure. The object starts moving towards the mirror at a speed 2 cm s-1. The position of the image from the mirror after 10 s will be at _____ cm.
solution:
In 10 s, the object will travel distance =2×10=20 cm

After 10 sec, the object will be at distance u=20-100=-80 m.

Focal length of the concave mirror is  f=R2=2002=-100 cm

Using mirror formula,

1v+1u=1f

⇒1v=-1100+180⇒v=800020

⇒v=400 cm

Position of image from the mirror is at 400 cm.

subject: Physics
q: Consider an equilateral prism (refractive index $\sqrt{2}$). A ray of light is incident on its one surface at a certain angle $i$. If the emergent ray is found to graze along the other surface then the angle of refraction at the incident surface is close to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Consider an equilateral prism (refractive index $\sqrt{2}$). A ray of light is incident on its one surface at a certain angle $i$. If the emergent ray is found to graze along the other surface then the angle of refraction at the incident surface is close to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Given an equilateral prism, the angle of the prism is $A = 60^{\circ}$.
The refractive index of the prism is $\mu = \sqrt{2}$.
The emergent ray grazes the second surface, which means the angle of emergence is $e = 90^{\circ}$.
Applying Snell's law at the second surface: $\mu \sin(r_2) = 1 \cdot \sin(e)$.
$\sqrt{2} \sin(r_2) = \sin(90^{\circ}) = 1$.
$\sin(r_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, which gives $r_2 = 45^{\circ}$.
For a prism, we know the relation $r_1 + r_2 = A$.
Substituting the values: $r_1 + 45^{\circ} = 60^{\circ}$.
$r_1 = 60^{\circ} - 45^{\circ} = 15^{\circ}$.
The angle of refraction at the incident surface is $r_1 = 15^{\circ}$.

subject: Physics
q: Two immiscible liquids of refractive indices 85 and 32 respectively are put in a beaker as shown in the figure. The height of each column is 6 cm. A coin is placed at the bottom of the beaker. For near normal vision, the apparent depth of the coin is α4 cm. The value of α is _______.
question: Two immiscible liquids of refractive indices 85 and 32 respectively are put in a beaker as shown in the figure. The height of each column is 6 cm. A coin is placed at the bottom of the beaker. For near normal vision, the apparent depth of the coin is α4 cm. The value of α is _______.
solution:
The formula to calculate the apparent height of the coin when seen through the combination of liquids can be written as

happ=h1μ1+h2μ2   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

happ=63/2+68/5 cm=4+154 cm=314 cm

Hence, α=31.

subject: Physics
q: A car is fitted with a convex side-view mirror of focal length $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. A second car $2.8 \mathrm{~m}$ behind the first car is overtaking the first car at relative speed of $15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The speed of the image of the second car as seen in the mirror of the first one is :
question: A car is fitted with a convex side-view mirror of focal length $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. A second car $2.8 \mathrm{~m}$ behind the first car is overtaking the first car at relative speed of $15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The speed of the image of the second car as seen in the mirror of the first one is :
solution:
$\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$-\frac{1}{v^2} \frac{d v}{d t}-\frac{1}{u^2} \frac{d u}{d t}=0$
$\frac{d v}{d t}=-\frac{v^2}{u^2}\left(\frac{d u}{d t}\right)$
$f=20 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\frac{1}{u}+\frac{1}{-280}=\frac{1}{20}$
$\Rightarrow v=\frac{280}{15} \mathrm{~cm}$
$v_1=-\left(\frac{280}{15 \times 280}\right)^2 \times 15$
$=\frac{1}{15} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q:
A convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and focal length 18 cm in air is immersed in water. The change in focal length of the lens will be_____cm. 

(Given refractive index of water=43)
question:
A convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and focal length 18 cm in air is immersed in water. The change in focal length of the lens will be_____cm. 

(Given refractive index of water=43)
solution:
From Lens Maker's formula,

For air: 1fa=μgμa-1 1R1-1R2

For water: 1fw=μgμw-1 1R1-1R2

∴    1fa1fw=μgμa-1μgμw-1

⇒fwfa=1.5-11.5×34-1⇒fw=0.50.125×18=72 cm

Thus, change in focal length of the lens is 72-18=54 cm

subject: Physics
q: A ray of light AO in vacuum is incident on a glass slab at angle 60° and refracted at angle 30° along OB as shown in the figure. The optical path length of light ray from A to B is:
question: A ray of light AO in vacuum is incident on a glass slab at angle 60° and refracted at angle 30° along OB as shown in the figure. The optical path length of light ray from A to B is:
solution:
Optical path length =OA+μOB
=acos 60o+μbcos 30o
=2a+μ2b3
From Snell’s law,
sin 60o=μ sin 30o
μ=3
Optical path length =2a+32b3
=2a+b

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to measure the focal length (f) of a convex lens, the magnitude of object distance(x) and the image distance(y) are measured with reference to the focal point of the lens. The y-x plot is shown in figure. The focal length of the lens is _____cm.
question: In an experiment to measure the focal length (f) of a convex lens, the magnitude of object distance(x) and the image distance(y) are measured with reference to the focal point of the lens. The y-x plot is shown in figure. The focal length of the lens is _____cm.
solution:
From Newton's formula for lens, we can write

x1x2=f2⇒20×20=f2

Therefore, f=20 cm

subject: Physics
q: A printed page is pressed by a glass of water. The refractive index of the glass and water is $1.5$ and $1.33$, respectively. If the thickness of the bottom of glass is $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and depth of water is $5 \mathrm{~cm}$, how much the page will appear to be shifted if viewed from the top ?
question: A printed page is pressed by a glass of water. The refractive index of the glass and water is $1.5$ and $1.33$, respectively. If the thickness of the bottom of glass is $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and depth of water is $5 \mathrm{~cm}$, how much the page will appear to be shifted if viewed from the top ?
solution:
Real depth $=5 \mathrm{~cm}+1 \mathrm{~cm}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$
Apparent depth $=\frac{\mathrm{d}_1}{\mu_1}+\frac{\mathrm{d}_2}{\mu_2}+\ldots$ $=\frac{5}{1.33}+\frac{1}{1.5}$ $\simeq 3.8+0.7 \simeq 4.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ $\therefore$ Shift $=6 \mathrm{~cm}-4.5 \mathrm{~cm} \cong 1.5 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q: Your friend is having eye sight problem. She is not able to see clearly a distant uniform window mesh and it appears to her as nonuniform and distorted. The doctor diagnosed the problem as :
question: Your friend is having eye sight problem. She is not able to see clearly a distant uniform window mesh and it appears to her as nonuniform and distorted. The doctor diagnosed the problem as :
solution: If distant objects are blurry then the problem is Myopia. If objects are distorted then the problem is Astigmatism

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statement is correct?
question: Which of the following statement is correct?
options: In primary rainbow, observer sees red colour on the top and violet on the bottom
In primary rainbow, observer sees violet colour on the top and red on the bottom
In primary rainbow, light wave suffers total internal reflection twice before coming out of water drops
Primary rainbow is less bright than secondary rainbow.
solution:
In primary rainbow, red colour is at top and violet is at bottom. The colours are in the order of violet, indigo, blue, green, yellow, orange and red (VIBGYOR). Since violet colour has the smallest wavelength, in the primary rainbow, it suffers maximum refraction. That is why it appears on the innermost position.

Intensity of secondary rainbow is less in comparison to primary rainbow.

subject: Physics
q: An object is placed on the principal axis of convex lens of focal length 10 cm as shown. A plane mirror is placed on the other side of lens at a distance of 20 cm. The image produced by the plane mirror is 5 cm inside the mirror. The distance of the object from the lens is ____ cm.
question: An object is placed on the principal axis of convex lens of focal length 10 cm as shown. A plane mirror is placed on the other side of lens at a distance of 20 cm. The image produced by the plane mirror is 5 cm inside the mirror. The distance of the object from the lens is ____ cm.
solution:
Given here: Focal length of convex lens, f=10 cm

Now, as seen in figure, image from lens is formed at point I1, whose location is 5 cm from mirror. Thus, I1 is at 15 cm from lens.

Using lens equation, we have

1v-1u=1f

Putting the value, 

115-1-u=110

⇒1u=110-115⇒u=10×1510-15

Thus, object is placed at 30 cm from lens.

q:
The angle of deviation through a prism is minimum when 

(A) Incident ray and emergent ray are symmetric to the prism

(B) The refracted ray inside the prism becomes parallel to its base

(C) Angle of incidence is equal to that of the angle of emergence

(D) When angle of emergence is double the angle of incidence

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
The angle of deviation through a prism is minimum when 

(A) Incident ray and emergent ray are symmetric to the prism

(B) The refracted ray inside the prism becomes parallel to its base

(C) Angle of incidence is equal to that of the angle of emergence

(D) When angle of emergence is double the angle of incidence

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
As per the figure given in question, we are considering it an Isosceles Prism.

Deviation is minimum in a prism when :

i=e,r1=r2 and ray 2 is parallel to base of prism.

subject: Physics
q: A short straight object of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> lies before the central axis of a spherical mirror whose focal length has absolute value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>f</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The image of object produced by the mirror is of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and has the same orientation of the object. One may conclude from the information:
question: A short straight object of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> lies before the central axis of a spherical mirror whose focal length has absolute value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>f</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The image of object produced by the mirror is of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and has the same orientation of the object. One may conclude from the information:
options: Image is real, same side of convex mirror.
Image is virtual, opposite side of concave mirror.
Image is real, same side of concave mirror.
Image is virtual, opposite side of convex mirror.
solution: Since orientation is same image is virtual. Since image is smaller the mirror has to be convex

subject: Physics
q: A rod of length $10$ cm lies along the principle axis of a concave mirror of focal length $10$ cm as shown in figure. The length of the image is _____ cm.
question: A rod of length $10$ cm lies along the principle axis of a concave mirror of focal length $10$ cm as shown in figure. The length of the image is _____ cm.
solution:
For the given concave mirror, the focal length is $f = -10$ cm.

The rod lies along the principal axis. From the figure, the near end of the rod is at a distance of $20$ cm from the pole of the mirror. Since the length of the rod is $10$ cm, the far end is at a distance of $20 + 10 = 30$ cm from the pole.

Let the near end be $A$ and the far end be $B$.
Object distance for end $A$, $u_A = -20$ cm.
Object distance for end $B$, $u_B = -30$ cm.

Using the mirror formula, $\dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

For end $A$:
$\dfrac{1}{v_A} + \dfrac{1}{-20} = \dfrac{1}{-10}$
$\dfrac{1}{v_A} = \dfrac{1}{20} - \dfrac{1}{10} = -\dfrac{1}{20}$
$v_A = -20$ cm

For end $B$:
$\dfrac{1}{v_B} + \dfrac{1}{-30} = \dfrac{1}{-10}$
$\dfrac{1}{v_B} = \dfrac{1}{30} - \dfrac{1}{10} = \dfrac{1 - 3}{30} = -\dfrac{2}{30} = -\dfrac{1}{15}$
$v_B = -15$ cm

The length of the image is the distance between the images of the two ends:
$\text{Length of image} = |v_A - v_B| = |-20 - (-15)| = |-5| = 5$ cm.

Answer: $5$

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens of focal length <math><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> produces images of the same magnification 2 when an object is kept at two distances <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> from the lens. The ratio of <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
question: A convex lens of focal length <math><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> produces images of the same magnification 2 when an object is kept at two distances <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> from the lens. The ratio of <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
solution:
Given, m=2⇒m=±2
From this lens formula, 1v+1u=1f
uv+1=uf
1m+1=uf
u=f1m+1
x1=201+12
x2=201-12
x1x2=31

subject: Physics
q: Light travels in two media <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with speeds <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The critical angle between them is
question: Light travels in two media <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with speeds <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The critical angle between them is
solution:
Relative refractive index can be found as,

μ2μ1=v1v2=1.52=34.

When the angle of incident is greater than the critical angle total internal reflection occurs. If the critical angle is ic then,

sinic=μ2μ1=34

Now tanic=342-32=37

Therefore, ic=tan-137

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> meter long scale with least count of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is used to measure the locations of objects on an optical bench. While measuring the focal length of a convex lens, the object pin and the convex lens are placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> mark and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> mark, respectively. The image of the object pin on the other side of lens coincides with image pin that is kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> mark. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math> error in the estimation of focal length is:
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> meter long scale with least count of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is used to measure the locations of objects on an optical bench. While measuring the focal length of a convex lens, the object pin and the convex lens are placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> mark and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> mark, respectively. The image of the object pin on the other side of lens coincides with image pin that is kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> mark. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math> error in the estimation of focal length is:
solution:
The formula for a lens is given by 1f=1v-1u   ...(i)

u=100±0.2 cm-80±0.2 cm=20±0.4 cm

v=180±0.2 cm-100±0.2 cm=80±0.4 cm

Substituting the values in the equation of focal length,

1f=1v-1u=180--120=580

⇒f=16 cm

Differentiating the equation (i)

dff2=dvv2+duu2(Only positive sign is considered for error calculation)

=0.46400+0.4400=0.4×176400

⇒df=0.4×176400×162=0.272

The percentage error is 

dff×100=0.27216×100=1.70%

subject: Physics
q: In a compound microscope, the magnified virtual image is formed at a distance of <math><mrow><mn>25</mn><mtext> cm</mtext></mrow></math> from the eye-piece. The focal length of its objective lens is <math><mrow><mn>1</mn><mtext> cm</mtext></mrow></math>. If the magnification is <math><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow></math> and the tube length of the microscope is <math><mrow><mn>20</mn><mtext> cm</mtext></mrow></math>, then the focal length of the eye-piece lens (in <math><mrow><mtext>cm</mtext></mrow></math>) is ______
question: In a compound microscope, the magnified virtual image is formed at a distance of <math><mrow><mn>25</mn><mtext> cm</mtext></mrow></math> from the eye-piece. The focal length of its objective lens is <math><mrow><mn>1</mn><mtext> cm</mtext></mrow></math>. If the magnification is <math><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow></math> and the tube length of the microscope is <math><mrow><mn>20</mn><mtext> cm</mtext></mrow></math>, then the focal length of the eye-piece lens (in <math><mrow><mtext>cm</mtext></mrow></math>) is ______
solution:
M=v0u01+Dfe

M=Lf01+Dfe

100=2011+25fe ; 5=1+25fe

4=25fe ; fe=254=6.25 cm
